coredata.dc:title: Insights into the newly synthesized N-doped carbon dots for Q235 steel corrosion retardation in acidizing media: A detailed multidimensional study
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Mengyue
Guo, Lei
He, Zhongyi
Marzouki, Riadh
Zhang, Renhui
Berdimurodov, Elyor
coredata.dc:description: N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using folic acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. The inhibition behaviour of the NCQDs on Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution was appraised through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), potentiodynamic polarization curves (PDP), and surface analysis. The results demonstrated that the synthesized NCQDs had an effective anticorrosion effect on Q235 steel, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency of 150 mg/L NCQDs reached 95.4%. Additionally, the analysis of the PDP corrosion potential changes indicated that the NCQDs acted as a mixed corrosion inhibitor. Moreover, the NCQDs adsorbed onto the surface of steel by coordinating its electron-rich atoms with the iron metal to form a protective film, which slowed the dissolution reaction of the anodic metal to achieve corrosion inhibition. The adsorption mechanism of the NCQDs was consistent with Langmuir adsorption, including physical and chemical adsorption. Therefore, this work can inspire and facilitate, to a certain extent, the future application of doped carbon quantum dots as efficient corrosion inhibitors in pickling solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Q235 steel
Corrosion inhibition
Electrochemistry
Mechanism

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the electrode and solutions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Properties and applications of carbon dots.
Schematic illustration for the preparation process of the designed NCQDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of the NCQDs and folic acid; (b) UV–vis spectra of the NCQDs, insets: NCQDs in sunlight and 365 nm light; and (c) and (d) XRD patterns and TEM images of the NCQDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of the NCQDs: (a) survey, (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, and (d) O 1 s.
Potentiodynamic polarization curves of the Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution: uninhibited and inhibited with different concentrations of NCQDs.
Nyquist and Bode plots of the Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of different concentrations of NCQDs at 298 K.
Employed equivalent circuit for fitting the impedance data.
SEM/EDS images of the surface on the Q235 steel: (a, d) polished, (b, e) blank, and (c, f) with 150 mg/L NCQDs in 1 M HCl.
(a) AFM and CAM of the Q235 steels under different conditions: polished and dipped in 1 M HCl solution and 150 mg/L NCQDs. (b) FTIR spectra of the steel surface with NCQDs and only the NCQDs.

XPS spectra of the Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution after 6 h of immersion without and with 150 mg/L NCQDs: (a) survey spectrum and (b) N 1 s spectrum of the steel surface in solution with 150 mg/L NCQDs.
Langmuir adsorption isotherm plot of the NCQDs on the Q235 steel interface.
The schematic diagram of corrosion protection of the Q235 steel dipped in 1 M HCl solution with the NCQDs.
Potentiodynamic polarization parameters of the Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl medium in the absence and presence of different concentrations of the NCQDs.
EIS fitting parameters of the Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of different concentrations of NCQDs at 298 K.

head.ce:title: Insights into the newly synthesized N-doped carbon dots for Q235 steel corrosion retardation in acidizing media: A detailed multidimensional study

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using folic acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. The inhibition behaviour of the NCQDs on Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution was appraised through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), potentiodynamic polarization curves (PDP), and surface analysis. The results demonstrated that the synthesized NCQDs had an effective anticorrosion effect on Q235 steel, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency of 150 mg/L NCQDs reached 95.4%. Additionally, the analysis of the PDP corrosion potential changes indicated that the NCQDs acted as a mixed corrosion inhibitor. Moreover, the NCQDs adsorbed onto the surface of steel by coordinating its electron-rich atoms with the iron metal to form a protective film, which slowed the dissolution reaction of the anodic metal to achieve corrosion inhibition. The adsorption mechanism of the NCQDs was consistent with Langmuir adsorption, including physical and chemical adsorption. Therefore, this work can inspire and facilitate, to a certain extent, the future application of doped carbon quantum dots as efficient corrosion inhibitors in pickling solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon steel is favoured and selected by professionals in different fields, such as the aviation, marine, construction steel, oil, and gas pipeline industries [1], because of its notable mechanical properties, excellent thermal conductivity, good electrical conductivity, and low cost [2]. However, carbon steel is prone to metal degradation due to the influence of different environmental media, which greatly reduces its service life. Moreover, this degradation has caused very large economic losses and serious environmental damage [3]. In industry, carbon steel is usually passivated by pickling treatment to improve its rust and corrosion resistance. However, a high concentration of pickling solution will aggravate the corrosion of carbon steel [4]. Therefore, researchers have developed different types of corrosion inhibitors, such as inorganic corrosion inhibitors, organic corrosion inhibitors, and biological corrosion inhibitors, to inhibit the corrosion behaviour of steel during pickling. Traditional inorganic corrosion inhibitors include chromate, phosphate, and nitrite [5]. Although the effect of these corrosion inhibitors is relatively significant, their use will cause pollution and problems such as the enrichment of heavy metals in aquatic environments. Therefore, researchers have gradually turned to the development of organic and biological corrosion inhibitors with low toxicity and low pollution. Biological corrosion inhibitors are often drugs, plant extracts, and biological enzymes [6–8] because of their low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Organic corrosion inhibitors are generally divided into aromatic ring molecules, surfactants, ionic liquids, etc. They usually contain electron-rich atoms such as S, N, and O so that they can coordinate with metals and adsorb on the surface to block the corrosive medium [9–12]. However, organic corrosion inhibitors still have some difficulties, such as the poor water solubility of organic molecules, complex synthesis processes, low synthesis efficiency, and toxicity. Therefore, researchers are constantly improving and developing these inhibitors to overcome this deficiency, with the expectation that a corrosion inhibitor with good water solubility, low toxicity, and low energy consumption will be found.

Carbon quantum dots have been widely studied in various fields, such as bioimaging [13], biosensing [14], battery storage [15], optical catalysis [16], fluorescent ink [17], and biomedicine [18]. This is because of their high water solubility, simple synthesis, easy modification, superior optical and electronic properties, outstanding fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility [19], and low toxicity (Fig. 1 ). Along with the research upsurge of the excellent performance of carbon quantum dots, carbon quantum dots have also attracted considerable attention from corrosion inhibitor researchers. Cen et al. [20] declared that the corrosion inhibition efficiency of 50 mg/L N, S-CDs on carbon steel reached 93% in a saturated CO2–3.5% NaCl solution. Qiang and collaborators [21] reported that NCDs synthesized by 4-aminosalicylic acid as corrosion inhibitors inhibited the corrosion behaviour of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Cao and coworkers [22] found that N-doped carbon quantum dots synthesized by citric acid and thiourea exhibited 95% corrosion inhibition efficiency on carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Ye and colleagues [23] proved that the prepared N-doped carbon dots exhibited excellent corrosion inhibition on Q235 steel in 0.1 M HCl solution. Saraswat and collaborators [24] demonstrated the corrosion inhibition effect of two doped carbon dots S, N co-doped (CD1) and N doped (CD2) on mild steel in 15% HCl solution. The results showed that the corrosion inhibition efficiencies of CD1 and CD2 at 100 ppm were 96.40% and 90.00%, respectively. Liu et al. [25] studied the corrosion inhibition performance of N-doped carbon dots synthesized at different hydrothermal temperatures on Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution. Electrochemical tests showed that the corrosion inhibition efficiencies of the three N-CDs were all greater than 90%. To date, although reports of doped carbon quantum dots as corrosion inhibitors have gradually increased, studies on corrosion inhibitors under different conditions are still rare, and the mechanism of carbon quantum as corrosion inhibitors is still unclear. Therefore, doped carbon quantum dots as corrosion inhibitors are in urgent need of many studies, and the corrosion inhibition mechanism also needs to be further explored.

In this work, we selected polyaromatic ring folic acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors to synthesize hydrosoluble N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) by a simple hydrothermal method. The chemical properties and morphology of the prepared NCQDs were evaluated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, ultraviolet–visible absorption (UV–vis) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The corrosion inhibition performance of NCQDs was estimated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization curve (PDP). The surface morphology, wettability, and chemical composition of Q235 steel after corrosion immersion were determined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), contact angle measurements (CAM), FTIR, and XPS. The adsorption mechanism of the NCQDs on the Q235 steel surface was investigated by Langmuir adsorption and the chemical composition of the adsorption surface.

Folic acid (AR) and o-phenylenediamine (AR) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (36 ∼ 38%) was obtained from Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The Q235 steels were purchased from local suppliers, and their composition (wt.%) was 0.17% C, 0.18% Si, 0.13% Mn, 0.40% P, 0.04% S, 0.025% Cu, and balance Fe.

Folic acid (0.6968 g) and o-phenylenediamine (0.6838 g) were completely dissolved by ultrasound in 100 mL of ultrapure water. The solution was added to a Teflon reactor and heated for 6 h at 200 °C. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, and the reactor was removed. Then, the orange–yellow solution was obtained through 0.45 μm vacuum filtration, and 0.22 μm microporous filtration. The unreacted small molecular compounds were removed by dialysis for 72 h with a 1000 molecular weight cut-off dialysis bag. Finally, the purified solution was freeze-dried for 3 days to obtain a brown–yellow solid. The reaction process is shown in Fig. 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the electrode and solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The Q235 steels (Φ16 × 5 mm) were polished by different grits of sandpaper (120 ∼ 1200) as the working electrode in the electrochemical test, ultrasonically cleaned with ultrapure water (made in the laboratory) and then anhydride ethanol, and dried with cold air. The HCl solution was prepared with concentrated hydrochloric acid as a blank control solution. Ultrapure water was used to dissolve the solid NCQDs and 1 M HCl solutions with different concentrations of NCQDs (10, 50, 100, and 150 mg/L) were prepared in volumetric flasks.

The structure of the NCQDs was investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Irtracer-100) and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometry (Tu-1901 Dual-beam). The bonding status of the NCQDs was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha). An Al Kα (hν = 1486.68 eV) excitation source was used for the XPS tests, and the C 1 s binding energy (284.80 eV) was used as the energy standard for correction. The crystal structure and morphology of the NCQDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD-6100) and transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2010).

Electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai, China) was used to record the potentiodynamic polarization curve (PDP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data. The corrosion inhibition effect of N-doped carbon quantum dots was tested by the traditional three electrode system. The three-electrode systems were composed of the Q235 steel with an exposed area of 1 cm2 as the working electrode, platinum electrode as the counter electrode, and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, respectively. Before the electrochemical test, the open circuit potential (OCP) of the reaction solution needed to be determined for 20 min under the condition of no current to detect the stable state of the reaction system. EIS tests were performed at the open circuit potential of the interference signal with an amplitude of 10 mV and the frequency range of 100 kHz ∼ 10 mHz. The EIS data was acquired from an equivalent circuit simulation with ZsimpWin 3.6 software. PDP tests were executed in a potential range of ± 250 mV versus the stable open circuit potential at a scanning rate of 0.5 mV/s. The whole test was carried out at room temperature. The experiment was repeated three times at the same concentration to reduce the experimental error, and the standard deviations (SD) were calculated.

To detect the surface morphology and composition of the Q235 steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution without inhibitor and containing 150 mg/L inhibitor for 6 h, scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL-JSM-7800F) and X-ray energy spectrum analysis (EDS) were both applied. The chemical composition and bonding form of the Q235 steel surface were obtained by FTIR spectroscopy and XPS. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, MFP-3D-BIO) was used to examine the surface roughness of the Q235 steel. The selected test sample was a 10 × 10 × 2 mm3 Q235 steel sheet. The wettability of the surface of Q235 steel was verified by a contact angle meter (CAM, SCI2000B).

The FTIR spectrums of NCQDs and folic acid are shown in Fig. 3 a. Regarding the NCQDs, the peaks at 3145.8 cm−1, 2351.2 cm−1, 1693.5 cm−1, 1516.0 cm−1, 1431.1 cm−1, and 754.16 cm−1 are attributed to − NH and − OH stretching vibrations, −CN stretching vibrations, CO stretching vibrations, NH bending vibrations, −COOH symmetric stretching vibrations and out-of-plane bending vibrations of aromatic rings, respectively [26,27]. In the case of folic acid, the peaks at 3115.0 cm−1, 1681.9 cm−1, 1477.4 cm−1, and 752.2 cm−1 correspond to − OH and − NH stretching vibrations, CO stretching vibrations, −C H shear vibrations and plane bending vibrations of aromatic rings [23]. The FTIR spectra of the NCQDs and folic acid were compared, showing that amide groups were produced by the acylation reaction and that a large number of aromatic rings were obtained by the carbonization reaction.

Fig. 3b shows the UV–vis spectrum of the NCQDs. The NCQDs exhibit three adsorption peaks at approximately 207, 247, and 371 nm, owing to the π–π* transition of CC bonds in aromatic rings, π-π* transition of conjugate systems and n-π* transition of conjugate molecules containing heterocyclic groups (CO, NN and − NH2) [28]. The inset in Fig. 3b shows that the colour of the NCQDs aqueous solution is orange under visible light irradiation and displays deep-blue fluorescence under excitation at a wavelength of 365 nm in the ultraviolet region. This result indicates that the NCQDs have a fluorescent property. Combined with the FTIR spectrum analysis, the XRD results of the N-doped carbon quantum dots show that they are synthesized by the polymerization and cyclization of the precursor. As shown in Fig. 3c, the XRD pattern of the NCQDs indicates a wide diffraction peak with a 2θ of 22.3° and a layer spacing of 0.394 nm, which is significantly larger than that of graphene (0.34 nm). These results illustrate that the introduction of N and O atoms in the NCQDs increases the interlayer spacing of amorphous carbon compounds [20]. The morphology and size of the NCQDs were characterized by TEM, as shown in Fig. 3d. The NCQDs exhibit an amorphous spherical structure of 2 ∼ 6 nm. Combined with the characterization of the structure and properties of the NCQDs, the results indicate that the carbonized NCQDs have good dispersion, quantum size and strong fluorescence characteristics.

The XPS spectra of the prepared NCQDs are shown in Fig. 4 . The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 4a) of the NCQDs reveals that they are composed of C, N and O atoms. By Gaussian fitting, the C 1 s spectrum in Fig. 4b shows three peaks, which are 284.28, 285.48, and 287.83 eV, corresponding to the C C single bond, the C O single bond and the unsaturated bond CO, respectively. The N 1 s spectrum in Fig. 4c shows two peaks, representing pyridinic N at 398.78 eV and pyrrolic N at 399.23 eV. The O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 4d displays two main peaks. The peak at 531.48 eV is assigned to the C O band, and the peak at 533.08 eV can be ascribed to the CO band [29,30]. The XPS results are consistent with the FTIR and UV–vis spectroscopy results.

Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) is an electrochemical test used to monitor the cathodic and anodic reactions of corrosion behaviour. The corrosion reaction mechanism was explained by analysing the related data of the cathode and anode. Fig. 5 shows the PDP curves of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution with different concentrations of NCQDs. Table 2 shows the corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), cathode slope (β c), anode slope (β a), and corrosion inhibition efficiency (ηPDP) parameters in the corrosion process. The value of η PDP is obtained from the equation below [31].
(1)
η PDP
( % )
=
i
corr
0
-
i
corr
′
i
corr
0
× 100

where i0 corr and
i corr ′
are the corrosion current densities in the uninhibited and inhibited conditions, respectively.

According to the measurement results of the PDP curves in Fig. 5, the trend of the cathode reaction and anode reaction curve after the addition of NCQDs inhibitor does not change compared with the blank solution. This result indicates that the introduction of NCQDs has no influence on the reaction mechanism process of the cathode and anode. The cathodic and anodic curves of the inhibitor shift downward compared with the 1 M HCl solution, and the corrosion current density decreases. This shows that NCQDs can increase the corrosion inhibition effect on Q235 steel. Table 1
reveals that the corrosion potential shifts in the positive direction as the concentration of the corrosion inhibitor increases. The maximum potential difference between 150 mg/L NCQDs (−0.468 V) and blank solution (−0.483 V) is less than 85 mV, suggesting that the NCQDs are a mixed-type inhibitor that inhibits not only the reduction reaction of the cathodic hydrogen ions but also the dissolution reaction of the anodic metals [32]. The corrosion current density data revealed that the value of NCQDs decreased with increasing NCQDs concentration, emphasizing that NCQDs adsorbed on the surface of the Q235 steel formed a film that inhibited the corrosion of the metal. The slopes of the anode and cathode were obtained by taking the corresponding tangents to the hyperbola and extending them to the point of intersection. Table 1 shows that the anode slope has a large fluctuation; that is, the electrochemical process is mainly inclined to inhibit the anodic reaction. The calculated corrosion inhibition efficiency also demonstrated that the NCQDs can be adsorbed on the surface of Q235 steel to reduce corrosion [33].

The Nyquist and Bode plots of the Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solutions with different concentrations of NCQDs are shown in Fig. 6 . The Nyquist curve shows a similar imperfect semicircular arc of capacitive reactance. The radius and height of the arc increase with increasing NCQDs concentration. This indicates that the introduction of the corrosion inhibitor increases the impedance and inhibits corrosion without altering the charge transfer reaction mechanism. The phase angle in the Bode plots shows that there is only one charge transfer relaxation time constant during the reaction. The impedance modulus of the Bode plot tends to increase when the inhibition concentration of the NCQDs increases. These results are attributed to the NCQDs inhibitor adsorbing on the surface of the Q235 steel, which blocks the electron transfer process and increases the capacitive reactance.

To explain the corrosion behaviour in detail, EIS simulation data were obtained by fitting the equivalent circuit diagram in Fig. 7 . The chi-square value (χ 2) is the goodness of fit. As shown in Table 2, the χ 2 value is within the range of 0.22 × 10−3 ∼ 1.72 × 10−3, indicating that the fitting data are in good agreement with the experimental data [34].

The equivalent circuit is composed of three simple components: R s is the solution resistance, R p is the polarization resistance, and CPE is the constant phase element. The fitting data is collected in Table 1. The impedance value of the CPE can be obtained by the following formula [35]:
(2)
Z CPE
=
Y 0
- 1
( j ω )
- n

where Y 0 refers to the magnitude of CPE, n represents the CPE exponent, j and ω are the imaginary value (j 2 = −1) and angular frequency (ω = 2πf), respectively. The n values can be −1, 0, 0.5, and 1, which represent an ideal inductor, a resistor, Warburg impedance, and a capacitor, respectively [36]. According to the n value in Table 1, the capacitance of the double electric layer (C dl) in the reaction process can be judged to be a nonideal element. Table 1 shows that the n values of the introduced corrosion inhibitor are lower than that of the blank solution. The n values show a gradually decreasing trend as the concentration of corrosion inhibitor increases. This is because the higher concentration of corrosion inhibitors adsorbed on the surface of Q235 steel is more likely to cause an increase in the surface roughness. The C dl values are acquired by [37]:
(3)
C dl
=
(
Y 0
R
p
1 - n
)
- n

In addition, the C dl can also be evaluated by the Helmholtz model equation [38]:
(4)
C dl
=
ε 0
ε
d
S

where
ε 0
and
ε
stand for the dielectric constant and the vacuum permittivity, respectively, S acts as the area of the electrode, and d represents the thickness of C dl. The C dl values in Table 1 indicate that the value after the addition of corrosion inhibitor is lower than the 1 M HCl solution. This is attributed to the formation of an uneven adsorption film between the NCQDs and Q235 steel surface during the adsorption and desorption process. As the thickness of the double electric layer increases, so does the capacitance; thus, the electron transfer process is prevented and a corrosion inhibition effect is achieved [39].

The corrosion inhibition efficiency (ηEIS) can be determined by [40]:
(5)
η EIS
%
=
R p ′
-
R
p
0
R p ′
× 100

where R0 p and
R
p
0
are the polarization resistance of the Q235 steel in the acidic solution without and with inhibitor, respectively. Table 2 shows that the corrosion inhibition efficiency of 150 mg/L NCQDs is 95.4%. This situation is attributed to the presence of NCQDs adsorption and water molecule desorption reactions on the Q235 surface [41], which prevent electron transfer and slows the metal dissolution rate. As a result, the corrosion efficiency of the metal is reduced to play a protective role.

To explore the durability of the corrosion inhibition performance of NCQDs, we explored the corrosion inhibition performance of NCQDs on the Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution within 24 h of immersion time. The electrochemical findings are shown in Figs. S1 ∼ S2 and Table S1. The results indicate that the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the NCQDs does not change significantly, thus indicating that the NCQDs can be regarded as durable and effective corrosion inhibitors.

The surface morphology and elemental distribution of Q235 in three different states are presented in Fig. 8 . The SEM image of only the polished Q235 steel surface (Fig. 8a) has not observed obvious corrosion pits or surface coverings. In contrast, the surface of the steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution for 6 h at room temperature (Fig. 8b) demonstrates severe cracks, pitting pits, and large amounts of corrosion deposits. On the other hand, a small amount of pitting and a compact adsorbent are observed on the surface immersed in blank solution containing 150 mg/L NCQDs (Fig. 8c). EDS analysis was used to confirm the composition of the matrix surface. The surface of the Q235 steel after sandpaper polishing is mainly composed of Fe and C elements (Fig. 8d). The surface of Q235 exposed to 1 M HCl solution (Fig. 8e) also contains chloride ions in addition to Fe and C elements, indicating that the covering on the pitted surface consists of ferric chloride compounds. The surface of the Q235 steel exposed to 150 mg/L corrosion inhibitor in 1 M HCl medium consists of Fe, C, Cl and N elements, as shown Fig. 8f. The surface of the Q235 steel exposed to 150 mg/L corrosion inhibitor in 1 M HCl solution consists of Fe, C, O and N elements. The presence of N element on the surface verifies that the NCQDs corrosion inhibitor adsorbs on the metal surface. Therefore, it is indicated that the corrosion inhibition mechanism is the formation of an adsorption film.

The roughness and wettability of the Q235 steel surface in different states, namely, polished and immersed in 1 M HCl solution without and with 150 mg/L NCQDs for 6 h at 298 K, are obtained by AFM images and contact angle images, as shown in Fig. 9 a. All experiments were repeated three times, and the standard deviation is shown in Fig. 9a. The roughness values in the three different states are 33.71 nm, 156.6 nm, and 96.73 nm. The corrosion of the Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution with the corrosion inhibitor is weaker than that in the blank solution, which leads to a decrease in the surface roughness and delays corrosion. Additionally, the contact angle of the polished base steel is 67°, the contact angle of the steel in 1 M HCl solution decreases to 26°, and the contact angle of the steel with the corrosion inhibitor is 40°, which is higher than that of the blank control. This confirms that the severe corrosion in 1 M HCl solution enhances the hydrophilicity of the surface. In contrast, the addition of the corrosion inhibitor decreases the corrosion and increases the hydrophobicity of the surface, which slows the dissolution reaction of Fe.

The FTIR spectra of the surface of the Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution with 150 mg/L NCQDs and only the NCQDs are shown in Fig. 9b. The FTIR spectrum of the Q235 steel in a blank solution with NCQDs shows that the broad peak at 3106 cm−1 is associated with − OH and − NH stretching vibrations. In addition, the peaks at 1670 cm−1 and 1526 cm−1 are assigned to − CO and − CN stretching vibrations [27]. Compared with the FTIR of NCQDs, the Fe-NCQD spectrum shows that the NCQDs are successfully adsorbed on the metal surface. The XPS spectra of the Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution after 6 h of immersion without and with 150 mg/L NCQDs (Fig. 10 ) were investigated to explore the adsorption bonds and the chemical composition of the adsorption layer. The full spectra of steel without and with the NCQDs inhibitor are shown in Fig. 10a. According to the full-XPS spectrum, the surface of Q235 steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution contains Fe, O and C. In contrast, the XPS spectrum with the introduction of NCQDs shows the other elements along with N. This phenomenon confirms the presence of the corrosion inhibitor molecules on the surface of the NCQDs immersed steel. The N 1 s spectrum (shown in Fig. 10b) can be Gaussian fitted into two peaks: 398.18 eV corresponds to the cyclized CN − bond and 399.0 eV is assigned to the N − Fe bond [22,42–44]. This conclusion implies that the NCQDs inhibitor adsorbs on the metal surface and reacts with the iron in the metal to form chelating chemical bonds. According to the surface analysis results, it can be inferred that physical and chemical reactions occur between the corrosion inhibitor and metal, and a dense hydrophobic adsorptive film is formed to prevent the dissolution of the metal in acidic solutions; thus, a protective role is achieved on the surface of the metal.

From the electrochemical measurements, it can be found that the introduction of the NCQDs corrosion inhibitor can reduce the corrosion rate of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution. Moreover, the surface analysis shows that the enhanced corrosion inhibition effect is mainly due to the NCQDs adsorbed on the metal surface, which form a protective film. Therefore, we used electrochemical impedance spectroscopy to obtain Langmuir adsorption isotherms with a good fitting degree. As shown in Fig. 11 , the correlation coefficient R 2 value of 0.0990 indicates that the corrosion inhibitor complies with the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, which is acquired using the following formula [45]:
(6)
C ads
θ
=
1
K ads
+
C inh

where C inh is the concentration of the inhibitor, K ads represents the equilibrium constant, and θ stands for the surface coverage. K ads can be calculated as 965.2 g/L (SD = 0.0041) from the isotherm intercept. The thermodynamic parameter standard Gibbs free energy (ΔG0 ads) of the reaction can be derived from K ads. ΔG0 ads can be obtained by the following equation [46]:
(7)
Δ
G
ads
0
= - R T ln 1000
K ads

where R and T represent the gas constant and absolute temperature, respectively, and 1000 is the concentration of water in solution (g/L). The value of ΔG0 ads in the studied NCQDs is calculated to be − 34.1 kJ/mol (SD = 0.0071). The negative ΔG0 ads indicates that the reaction of the corrosion inhibitor on the metal surface is a spontaneous process. Furthermore, when ΔG0 ads is −20 kJ/mol or more, the process is considered physical adsorption; in contrast, When ΔG0 ads is −40 kJ/mol or less, the reaction process is deemed chemisorption. Therefore, when the ΔG0 ads is between −20 kJ/mol and −40 kJ/mol, the adsorption modes of the reaction process include physical and chemical adsorption [47]. This suggests that the adsorption modes of the studied NCQDs corrosion inhibitor include both physical and chemical adsorption.

The schematic diagram of the corrosion protection of the Q235 steel dipped in 1 M HCl solution with NCQDs is shown in Fig. 12 . According to the potentiodynamic polarization curve, the studied NCQDs inhibitor is a mixed inhibitor, which affects both the anodic and cathodic reactions. Therefore, based on this, we speculate that the mechanism of the dissolution reaction of the anodic Fe and the reduction reaction of the cathodic H+ is as follows [48]:

The anodic reaction mechanism with NCQDs:
(8)
Fe ·
H 2
O ads
+ Inh ↔
FeOH
ads
-
+
H
+
+ Inh
(9)
Fe ·
H 2
O ads
+ Inh → Fe ·
Inh ads
+
H 2
O
(10)
FeOH
ads
-
→
FeOH
ads
+
e
-
(11)
Fe ·
Inh ads
↔ Fe ·
Inh
ads
+
+
e
-
(12)
FeOH ads
+ Fe ·
Inh
ads
+
↔
FeOH
+
+ Fe ·
Inh ads
(13)
FeOH
+
+
H
+
↔
Fe
2 +
+
H 2
O

The cathodic reaction mechanism with NCQDs:
(14)
2H
+
+ 2
e
-
=
H 2
↑

Combined with the XPS results, the NCQDs corrosion inhibitor coordinates with the metal iron in 1 M HCl solution to form an Fe·inhads protective film, effectively preventing the dissolution of Fe and the dissociation of iron ions. Moreover, electron transfer is reduced due to inhibiting iron dissolution; thus, the reduction reaction of hydrogen ions is also inhibited [49]. Therefore, the NCQDs corrosion inhibitor plays effectively inhibits corrosion.

In conclusion, the research and development of new carbon quantum dots as corrosion inhibitors have become the focus of scientific researchers. In many works, researchers used aminosalicylic acid [26], o-phenylenediamine, p-phenylenediamine [50], and tryptophan [51] as precursors to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots. The corrosion inhibition performance of the above prepared NCQDs on carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution were studied. Their results indicated that the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots had a certain corrosion inhibition effect, but there were disadvantages such as a long preparation time and a low corrosion inhibition efficiency. Therefore, we used folic acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors to shorten the hydrothermal preparation time to 6 h and successfully prepared a new type of NCQDs. To explore the corrosion inhibition performance of the prepared NCQDs, we conducted a detailed multidimensional study. The PDP curves confirmed that the NCQDs on the Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution acted as a mixed corrosion inhibitor dominated by anodic inhibition. The EIS results proved that the adsorbed NCQDs on the Q235 steel surface formed a barrier film that effectively blocked electron transfer. When the concentration of NCQDs was 150 mg/L, the inhibition efficiency reached 95.4%. Therefore, the NCQDs prepared here were inhibitors with high anticorrosion properties. The adsorption of the NCQDs on the Q235 steel surface followed Langmuir adsorption. The adsorption mechanism showed that the NCQDs adsorbed on the metal surface formed a compact protective film, efficaciously preventing the dissolution reaction of anodic iron and effectively inhibiting the reduction reaction of cathodic hydrogen ions. Hence, NCQDs can reduce the corrosion efficiency of Q235 steel. In general, our work showed the preparation of a new type of NCQDs that had good water solubility and efficient anticorrosion properties. Therefore, this study provides new ideas for the future selection and preparation of NCQDs and guidance for the corrosion inhibition mechanism of NCQDs.

Mengyue Zhu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Lei Guo: Supervision, Project administration. Zhongyi He: Conceptualization, Visualization. Riadh Marzouki: Software, Methodology. Renhui Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Elyor Berdimurodov: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Riadh Marzouki extends his appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for partly funding this work through research groups under grant number R.G.P.2/129/42.

This work was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52074232, 22062022), the Foundation of the Department of Education of Guizhou Province (QJHKY[2020]067, KY[2018]030), and the Opening Project of the Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Material Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (2020CL06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.10.160.

ce:section.ce:para:
